aliases: deep-fried prawns with oat cereal coating

english: deep-fried prawns with oat cereal coating

food_subgroup: Deep-fried then butter-wok-fried whole prawns with processed oat cereal (Nestum) coating

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for cereal butter prawns as a distinct dish. Closest parent concept: fried prawn dish (FOODON_03301710 — shrimp/prawn food product). No exact match available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans — cooked (closest FoodEx2 parent for cooked prawn dish; no specific code for cereal butter prawns preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A039V (Crustaceans — cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No FoodEx2 code exists for the specific cereal-butter-fried preparation style.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for cereal butter prawns. SNOMED CT food coding is not applicable for this composite zi char dish.

main_ingredients: Nestum oat cereal (processed sweetened oat powder)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char restaurant portion of cereal butter prawns is approximately 200g, comprising roughly 120g of deep-fried whole prawns plus approximately 50g of Nestum-butter cereal coating, with curry leaves, egg floss, and chilli padi making up the remainder.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal Butter Prawns — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central component cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cereal butter prawns (200g zi char portion). Per-100g values derived by proportional calculation from the 200g serving reference. Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy: shrimp, cooked; butter; oat cereal) to validate macro and micronutrient plausibility. Values represent the composite cooked dish including deep-fried prawns and Nestum-butter coating.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Oat-Based Cereals and Processed Grain Products — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cereal butter prawns as a composite dish. GI ~62 (medium) is estimated using Nestum processed oat cereal as the primary carbohydrate source. Nestum is a processed, partially sweetened oat-grain powder (Nestlé); processed oat cereals have published GI values of approximately 55–70 in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., instant oat porridge GI ~66, rolled oats GI ~55). GI ~62 represents a midpoint estimate for a processed oat cereal product. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 28) / 100 = 17.4. The high fat content (28g per 200g serving) from butter and deep-frying moderates the postprandial glucose response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 200mg per 100g (400mg per 200g serving) from salted butter and seasoning, with cumulative dietary sodium risk from zi char meal context
hypertension.guidance: A 200g serving of cereal butter prawns contributes approximately 400mg sodium — about 17% of the 2,300mg daily limit — which is moderate for a single zi char dish, but patients with hypertension should account for cumulative sodium from other dishes in the same meal and limit portion size to one serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~17.4 per 200g serving) from Nestum processed oat cereal, partially offset by high fat content slowing gastric emptying; long-term insulin resistance risk from saturated fat (~10g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 17.4 per 200g serving is moderate for Type 2 Diabetes management; however, the high saturated fat content (~10g per serving from butter) poses a long-term insulin resistance risk, and patients with T2DM should limit cereal butter prawns to occasional consumption and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the overall meal glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14g/100g; 28g per serving) and saturated fat (5g/100g; 10g per serving) from butter and deep-frying, plus elevated cholesterol (120mg/100g; 240mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cereal butter prawns is a high cardiovascular concern dish due to its combination of deep-frying and butter-tossing, delivering approximately 28g total fat and 10g saturated fat per 200g serving — representing 50% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit — and patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should treat this as an occasional indulgence and avoid regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~10g per serving) promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; selenium from prawns (~44mcg per serving) provides a genuine anti-inflammatory micronutrient benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware that the high saturated fat content from butter (~10g per 200g serving) may exacerbate systemic inflammation, while the selenium content from prawns (~44mcg per serving, approximately 80% of the RDI) provides a genuine anti-inflammatory benefit; net assessment favours limiting frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal Butter Prawns (麦片奶油虾, Màipiàn Nǎiyóu Xiā) is a popular Singapore zi char dish invented in the 1990s that consistently misleads health-conscious diners. The word 'cereal' implies a wholesome grain component, but the cereal is Nestum — a processed, partially sweetened oat-grain powder by Nestlé — wok-fried in generous amounts of butter until golden and crispy. The preparation involves two cooking stages: whole prawns are first deep-fried (absorbing approximately 10–15g of fat), then tossed in a wok with melted butter, Nestum oat powder, egg floss, fresh curry leaves, and sliced chilli padi, creating a fragrant, crunchy, buttery, and slightly sweet coating. Per standard 200g zi char portion: approximately 450 kcal, 28g total fat (10g saturated fat from butter), 28g carbohydrate (GL ~17.4), and 24g protein. The dish is comparable in calorie and fat density to fried chicken wings. Clinically, patients who order cereal butter prawns as a 'lighter option' are nutritionally mistaken — the dual cooking method (deep-fry then butter-wok-fry) makes this one of the highest-fat prawn preparations at Singapore zi char stalls. Not halal (contains dairy butter). For Type 2 Diabetes: moderate glycemic load from Nestum oat powder, but high saturated fat poses long-term insulin resistance risk. For cardiovascular disease: high concern due to saturated fat and cholesterol load. Selenium from prawns (~44mcg per 200g serving, ~80% RDI) is a genuine nutritional benefit. Zinc (2mg per serving) supports immune function. The dish is typically served as a shared plate at zi char restaurants, seafood hawker stalls, and celebratory group meals.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cereal butter prawns with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central component data. The dish is a composite preparation with variable butter and oil absorption depending on restaurant; per-100g values represent a reasonable central estimate for a standard zi char preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on Nestum processed oat cereal as the carbohydrate source using University of Sydney GI Database reference values for processed oat cereals. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown and water content are not available from the primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite cereal butter prawns dish (deep-fried prawns + butter-Nestum coating) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g reported as 0.1g/100g from butter (trace dairy trans-fat, primarily vaccenic acid); this is a naturally occurring trace amount, not industrially produced trans-fat. Halal status: NOT halal — contains dairy butter. The 'cereal' in the dish name refers to Nestum (Nestlé processed oat powder), not a whole-grain health food; this is a common patient misconception. Cholesterol is elevated at 120mg/100g (240mg per 200g serving) primarily from prawns and egg floss. GI estimated at ~62 (medium) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous values for processed oat cereals; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Selenium content (~22mcg/100g; ~44mcg per 200g serving) from prawns represents approximately 80% of the adult RDI and is a genuine nutritional benefit of this dish.
